beats.narration: Radiography runs on arithmetic — exposure times, decayed sources, boundary distances. The same numbers that set your image also set your dose.

payload.title: RADIOGRAPHY MATH
payload.sub: The arithmetic behind every exposure and every boundary.

beats.narration: Your kit is four tools: exponents, square roots, scientific notation, and rearranging formulas — each tied to a calculation radiographers run daily.

payload.heading: Four tools you'll use every shift

beats.narration: Radiography's favorite exponent is the halving pattern: each half-life multiplies activity by one half. Two half-lives take 80 curies to 40, then 20.
Double the distance and intensity falls to one quarter. Run it backward with a square root, and the law sets your safety boundary distance.

payload.heading: The inverse square law, run backward
payload.note: SOLVING FOR DISTANCE → TAKE A SQUARE ROOT

beats.narration: Show the work, then check it back. A math slip here isn't a red mark — it can be a dose you can't take back.

payload.sub: A math slip can be a dose you can't take back.
